questions.question: Explain the role of an Operating System as a resource manager. What resources does it manage?
keyPoints: OS coordinates allocation of CPU time among competing processes
Manages primary memory (RAM) allocation to programs
Controls secondary storage (disk) space and file organization
Handles I/O devices like printers, keyboards, monitors
Ensures fair and efficient resource allocation using scheduling algorithms

questions.question: What is multiprogramming? How does it improve CPU utilization?
keyPoints: Multiprogramming loads multiple programs in memory simultaneously
CPU switches between programs when one waits for I/O
When a program performs I/O operation, CPU executes another ready program
Keeps CPU busy most of the time instead of waiting idle
Increases overall system throughput and efficiency

questions.question: Differentiate between Batch Operating System and Time-Sharing Operating System.
keyPoints: Batch OS processes jobs in groups (batches) without user interaction
Time-Sharing OS allows multiple users to interact with the computer simultaneously
Batch OS was used in early computing; Time-Sharing emerged in third generation
Time-Sharing uses CPU time-slicing to allocate small time quantum to each user
Time-Sharing provides faster response and supports interactive applications

questions.question: What are system calls? Give two examples of system calls and explain their purpose.
keyPoints: System calls provide interface between user programs and OS kernel
They allow programs to request privileged services from the operating system
Examples: fork() - creates a new process, exec() - replaces current process image
read()/write() - file operations, open() - opens a file for access
System calls operate in privileged mode and maintain system security

questions.question: Describe the boot process of a computer system.
keyPoints: Power-On Self-Test (POST) checks basic hardware functionality
BIOS/UEFI firmware initializes and tests hardware components
Boot loader is loaded from Master Boot Record (MBR) or EFI partition
Boot loader locates and loads the OS kernel into memory
Kernel initializes system services and starts background processes
Finally, user interface (login screen/desktop) is displayed

questions.question: What is the difference between a monolithic kernel and a microkernel architecture?
keyPoints: Monolithic kernel has all OS services (file system, memory, process) in kernel space
Microkernel has minimal services in kernel space, others run in user space
Microkernel provides better reliability - failure of one service does not crash system
Monolithic kernel is faster due to less context switching
Examples: Linux and Unix use monolithic; Windows NT and macOS use hybrid approaches

questions.question: Define virtualization. How does it benefit modern computing environments?
keyPoints: Virtualization creates virtual instances of hardware resources
Allows multiple operating systems to run on a single physical machine
Hypervisor (VMM) manages resource allocation between virtual machines
Benefits: cost savings, isolation, server consolidation, easier backup
Enables cloud computing and efficient resource utilization

questions.question: Explain the five major functions of an Operating System in detail. For each function, describe its purpose and key activities.
keyPoints: PROCESS MANAGEMENT: Creates/deletes processes, schedules CPU time, handles inter-process communication, includes process control blocks and scheduling algorithms
MEMORY MANAGEMENT: Allocates/deallocates memory space, tracks memory usage, implements virtual memory, swaps processes between RAM and disk
FILE MANAGEMENT: Creates/deletes files and directories, manages file organization, controls access permissions, handles file operations (read, write, open, close)
DEVICE MANAGEMENT: Manages I/O devices through drivers, handles device scheduling, performs buffer management, provides device independence to users
SECURITY AND PROTECTION: Implements access control, authenticates users, protects data from unauthorized access, enforces permissions on resources

questions.question: Discuss the evolution of Operating Systems through different generations, highlighting key developments in each generation.
keyPoints: FIRST GENERATION (1940s-1950s): No OS, manual operation, plugboards for programming, no features
SECOND GENERATION (1950s-1960s): Batch processing systems introduced, FORTRAN and COBOL languages, card readers used
THIRD GENERATION (1960s-1970s): Multiprogramming and time-sharing introduced, integrated circuits, IBM OS/360
FOURTH GENERATION (1970s-1990s): Personal computers, GUI introduced (Xerox PARC, Apple Macintosh), MS-DOS, Unix, Windows
FIFTH GENERATION (1990s-present): Distributed systems, internet explosion, mobile OS (iOS, Android), cloud computing, AI integration

questions.question: What are the main types of Operating Systems? Explain each type with suitable examples and characteristics.
keyPoints: BATCH OS: Jobs prepared offline, processed in batches, no user interaction, used in payroll processing - example: early IBM systems
MULTIPROGRAMMING OS: Multiple programs in memory, CPU switches between them, better utilization - example: IBM OS/360
TIME-SHARING OS: Multiple users share CPU simultaneously, quick response time, supports interactivity - example: Unix, MULTICS
REAL-TIME OS: Guaranteed response within deadlines, used in embedded systems - example: VxWorks, QNX
DISTRIBUTED OS: Multiple computers work as single system, network-based - example: Amoeba, Plan 9
NETWORK OS: Manages network resources, provides file/print sharing - example: Novell NetWare, Windows Server

questions.question: Explain the concepts of User Mode and Kernel Mode in operating systems. Why is this distinction necessary? How does the system switch between these modes?
keyPoints: KERNEL MODE (privileged mode): OS runs with full hardware access, can execute privileged instructions, has access to all memory
USER MODE: Applications run with limited access, cannot directly access hardware or memory, uses system calls to request OS services
NEED FOR DISTINCTION: Prevents user programs from corrupting system, ensures security and stability, allows OS to control hardware access
SWITCHING: Achieved through interrupts (hardware signals), system calls (software interrupts), and traps
CPU uses mode bit in status register to track current execution mode, switches from user to kernel on interrupt/system call
